summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – LEGAL PRACTITIONERS – Where the father is in receipt of legal aid - Conduct of principal solicitor – Where a legal representative for the father holds a restricted practising certificate and is not an assigned practitioner on the legal aid (family law) panel –Consideration of whether a non-panel member (family law) can undertake work and/or appear in family law proceedings under the supervision of an assigned panel member (family law) – Where the principal solicitor has routinely authorised junior practitioners who are not panel members (family law) to appear in family law proceedings without direct in court supervision – Referral to the Legal Profession Conduct Commissioner and the Legal Services Commissioner FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Parenting proceedings – Where there was no appearance by any legal representative for the father resulting in an adjournment of proceedings–– Oral application for costs thrown away made by the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer - Costs order made against the father’s legal representative

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

These reasons relate to two separate procedural hearings in parenting proceedings, in which the court holds concerns about the conduct of the father’s solicitor on record, Mr Damien Andrew O’Dea.
The issues traversed in these reasons are of significant concern to the court for a range of reasons, including but not limited to the ethical obligations of practitioners appearing before the court, the confidence the court inherently reposes in legal practitioners, access to justice for parties who have the benefit of a grant of legal aid, and the professional obligations of senior practitioners towards junior practitioners.
The court derives no joy from dealing with conduct issues relating to legal practitioners. It is however a necessary responsibility of the court as it ensures public confidence in the legal system and the protection of litigants.
As observed by Spigelman CJ in New South Wales Bar Association v Cummins [2001] NSWCA 284; (2001) 52 NSWLR 279 at [19]- [20]:
19. ... The legal profession has long required the highest standards of integrity.
There are four interrelated interests involved. Clients must feel secure in confiding their secrets and entrusting their most personal affairs to lawyers. Fellow practitioners must be able to depend implicitly on the word and behaviour of their colleagues. The judiciary must have confidence in those who appear before the courts. The public must have confidence in the legal profession by reason of the central role the profession plays in the administration of justice. Many aspects of the administration of justice depend on the trust by the judiciary and/or the public in the performance of professional obligations by professional people.
For all of the reasons that follow orders shall be made referring Mr O’Dea to both the Director of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia and to the South Australian Legal Profession Conduct Commissioner.

BRIEF BACKGROUND

It is not necessary to traverse the details of the substantive parenting proceedings before the court, other than to identify the following:
(a) The current proceedings were commenced by the applicant father on 14 June 2024 in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 2. At that time, the father was represented by solicitors who are different to his current representation.

(b) The current proceedings are the second time that these parties have litigated parenting arrangements, with final parenting orders initially made in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 2, on 2 September 2020 and 4 December 2020.

(c) At a hearing on 10 October 2025 the proceedings were transferred to this court.

(d) Throughout the current litigation, the father has changed his legal representation several times, with his current legal representative Mr Damien Andrew O’Dea of O’Dea Lawyers filing a Notice of Address for Service on 17 October 2025 shortly after the transfer of the proceedings to this court.

(e) On 15 December 2025 orders were made in chambers listing the proceedings for a case management hearing on 28 January 2026. Those orders included orders for the filing of a Case Outline by each of the parties, together with Costs Notices.

(f) The father complied with the filing of a Case Outline by virtue of a document filed on 21 January 2026. That document included a statement that “the father is in receipt of legal aid”, and that he anticipated his trial costs would be met “under s102NA funding”. Whilst not explained or particularised in that document, the court understands this to mean a grant of funding advanced pursuant to the Commonwealth Family Violence and Cross Examination of Parties Scheme (“the Scheme”). To that end, during the hearing on 9 February 2026, Mr O’Dea reminded the court that a declaration had been made by Judge Brown on 10 October 2025 (prior to the transfer of the proceedings to this court) that the ban on cross-examination would apply to the final hearing of these proceedings. Whilst a little less clear, Mr O’Dea also informed the court at the hearing on 9 February 2026 (albeit less confidently) that the father’s present funding is pursuant to a grant of legal aid and that the father is not currently privately funding his legal representation in these proceedings.

THE HEARING ON 28 JANURAY 2026

The case management hearing which took place on 28 January 2026 ought be seen in some context, namely:
(a) The court had a busy case management list of four separate proceedings at intervals of approximately 30 minutes commencing at 2.30pm.

(b) These proceedings were the final of those four case management hearings.

(c) Ms Fuda of Counsel had appeared as counsel at the hearing immediately preceding the hearing in these proceedings.

When the hearing in these proceedings commenced, Ms Fuda appeared at the bar table as a courtesy and “friend of the court” and in lieu of a junior practitioner from Mr O’Dea’s office, Mr E, who was sitting in the body of the court room with the father and made no attempt to appear at the bar table.
When taking appearances, the court was informed by Ms Fuda that she was appearing in circumstances where she had been informed by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer that there was some difficulty in the junior practitioner from Mr O’Dea’s office appearing for the father in circumstances where the junior practitioner held a restricted practicing certificate (Category C), and was not a member of the legal aid panel of approved practitioners able to accept instructions and appear in family law proceedings.
In addition, the duty lawyer from the Legal Services Commission of South Australia appeared and ultimately advised the court that her understanding was that a practitioner holding a restricted practicing certificate would not ordinarily be a member of the legal aid panel of approved practitioners able to accept instructions and appear in family law proceedings pursuant to a grant of legal aid. There was however some ambiguity as to whether such a non‑ family law panel member could appear with their supervisor either sitting next to them at the bar table or in the body of the court room ostensibly supervising the appearance of the junior practitioner.
Ultimately the proceedings were necessarily adjourned in light of the fact that the junior practitioner did not appear for the father and as such there was no appearance by any legal representative for the father at the hearing, despite the court being advised that he was in receipt of a grant of legal aid.
In addition to adjourning the case management hearing, the court made orders listing the proceedings for a “show cause” event as to whether Mr O’Dea’s conduct (in failing to appear and/or supervise the junior practitioner, together with having a non-family law panel member appear) ought be referred to the Legal Profession Conduct Commissioner and/or given the grant of legal aid, to the Director of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia, together with consideration of whether a cost order ought be made personally against Mr O’Dea in relation to the costs thrown away of the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer.

THE HEARING ON 9 FEBRUARY 2026

In advance of the hearing on 9 February 2026, and on 8 February 2025, Mr O’Dea filed written submissions. The court has had regard to the same.
Mr O’Dea appeared at the hearing and indicated to the court that he both appeared for the father and in addition appeared for himself in relation to the show cause event. The father confirmed to the court (as he had done at the hearing on 28 January 2026) that he wished for Mr O’Dea to continue to represent him regardless of whether the court proceeded to make orders referring him to the Legal Profession Conduct Commissioner and/or the Director of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia. The junior practitioner, Mr E was also present at the hearing and sitting in the body of the court room.
At the hearing the court first dealt with the necessary case management arrangements. No more needs to be said about the same.
So far as the show cause event was concerned, the court heard oral submissions from all parties, together with some brief submissions from Mr Bourne (the Director of the Family Law Division of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia) who was in the body of the court room, but was called to the bar table to assist the court. Mr Bourne however was unfortunately unable to assist the court as to the specific arrangements and policies of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia as to whether a non-panel member could undertake work and/or appear in family law proceedings with the supervision of a panel member.
Following the hearing, the court received with the consent of all parties a copy of communication sent by Mr Bourne to Mr O’Dea on 5 February 2026 (Exhibit “F1”). That communication appears to have been generated in response to Mr O’Dea’s enquiry following the events which took place at the hearing on 28 January 2026. The communication however is of no assistance to the court as it simply referred Mr O’Dea in the most broad terms to “any relevant legislation, the practising certificate held, professional conduct rules, and court rules”.
During the hearing and of the court’s own accord, regard has been had to:
(a) The Rules of the Legal Practitioners Education and Admission Council 2018 (SA) (“the LPEAC Rules”), which are made pursuant to the Legal Practitioners Act 1981 (SA);

(b) The Legal Services Commission of South Australia website;

(c) The “Practitioner Panel Registry” available on the Legal Services Commission of South Australia website; and

(d) The General Panel Agreement prepared by the Legal Services Commission of South Australia and signed by Panel Approved Members.

It apparent from his submissions that Mr O’Dea is of the view that he is permitted to authorise junior practitioners from his firm, who are not family law panel members to undertake work and appear in family law proceedings under his supervision, which implicitly included court appearances without his direct presence and direct supervision.
Further, during the hearing Mr O’Dea acknowledged that he has routinely authorised junior practitioners who are not family law panel members to appear in family law proceedings in both the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 1 and the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 2.
When seeking clarity from Mr O’Dea as to how he came to have the understanding that he could authorise the appearance of junior practitioners who were not family law panel members, and when taken to the various documents before the court and referred to in his written submissions, Mr O’Dea repeatedly submitted that there was nothing in the policies and guidelines that specifically prohibited him doing so, whilst simultaneously acknowledging that there was nothing in the policies and guidelines that authorised him doing so.
This submission was in and of itself somewhat concerning, as it suggests:
(a) Firstly, that until raised by the court in these proceedings, there had been no attempt to seek clarity about these matters from the Legal Services Commission of South Australia before embarking on this course; and

(b) Secondly, a cavalier attitude towards professional obligations and specifically those obligations imposed on legal practitioners in the South Australian Legal Practitioners Conduct Rules 2022 (SA), and by the Legal Services Commission of South Australia upon those practitioners representing persons pursuant to a grant of legal aid.

THE LEGAL FRAMEWORK

Practising Certificate Requirements

The Legal Practitioners Education and Admission Council (“LPEAC”), established pursuant to Part 2A, Division 1 of the Legal Practitioners Act 1981 (SA) (“the LPA”), functions, to amongst other things, regulate the admission of practitioners who practice as a barrister or solicitor in the state of South Australia.
The LPEAC Rules, provide different categories of practicing certificates to be issued pursuant to s 16 of the LPA.
The court has been advised that the junior practitioner, Mr E, holds a “Category C” restricted practising certificate. Rule 10 of the LPEAC Rules defines such a practicing certificate as follows:
10 Categories of practising certificates

...
Category C: an employee practising certificate which enables the practitioner to undertake:
(i) work of an employed practitioner on a supervised basis pursuant to rule 11 (a restricted practising certificate)...
The LPEAC Rules relevantly set out the following in relation to “supervised practice”:
5 Supervised practice
(1) For the purposes of these rules, practice by a practitioner qualifies as supervised practice if–
...
the work of the practitioner is controlled or managed by a legal practitioner who has been in practice for at least 5 years preceding the commencement of the proposed supervised practice and who holds a Category A or Category B or unrestricted Category C practising certificate during the period of supervised practice (the supervising practitioner); and
during the period of practice–
(i) the practitioner is employed or engaged by the supervising practitioner; or

(ii) the practitioner is employed and the supervising practitioner is employed or engaged by the same person; or

(iii) the practitioner is employed and the supervising practitioner is employed or engaged to perform the work of a legal practitioner in the same practice; and

during the period of supervised practice the practitioner and the supervising practitioner work, or substantially work, at the same location.
...
(3) During the period of supervision, the practitioner and the supervising practitioner must, insofar as is reasonably practicable, comply with any guidelines issued by LPEAC for the purposes of this rule.

(4) If a practitioner is subject to a condition which requires that the practitioner must not practice without supervision, the requirement for supervision will not cease until the Board of Examiners is satisfied that there has been adequate compliance with that condition such that it is appropriate for the person to be permitted to practice without supervision.

LPEAC have issued guidelines for supervised practice pursuant to r 5(3) of the LPEAC Rules, with those guidelines publicly available, including on the Law Society of South Australia’s website. Those guidelines relevantly provide:
2. General Principles

2.1 Professional supervision and the duty to supervise

Supervision in a professional context is a relational process that encompasses aspects of risk management, training, mentoring and interpersonal support. A general duty to supervise is set out in Rule 37 of the Australian Solicitors Conduct Rules 2011 (SA) as follows:
A solicitor with designated responsibility for a matter must exercise reasonable supervision over solicitors and all other employees engaged in the provision of the legal services for that matter.
The scope of this duty hinges on the meaning of “reasonable”, and this depends on the context and specific circumstances in which supervision is being exercised. These requirements do not derogate from this general duty to supervise, or the need for robust practice management and risk management processes. Rather, supervised practice should be viewed as a unique context with additional considerations.

2.2 Supervised practice: a developmental context

Newly admitted legal practitioners must complete a two-year period of supervised practice before being eligible to hold an unrestricted practising certificate. This period of supervised practice is a unique supervisory context characterised by a supervisee who has limited practical experience. In this context, the supervisee’s professional development is critical and relies on specific training and support measures enabling a transition to unrestricted practice. This requires both the supervisor and the supervisee to have realistic expectations about what knowledge and skill set the supervisee is equipped with upon admission as a legal practitioner. In particular, supervisees at the beginning of their supervised practice period will have very limited legal practice experience and are in need of tailored training that progressively develops their level of competence.

...

3.2 Recognise the developmental context when delegating work

Supervisory processes should strike a balance between the supervisor’s time restraints and the developmental needs of the supervisee. Here it is important to remember that supervised practice is the final stage of legal education before unrestricted practice. This context is a critical consideration when delegating work and managing task completion.

For example, when delegating work, supervisors should avoid adopting the attitude that it would be quicker or easier to do the work themselves. Supervisors should also avoid simply dumping the supervisee with tasks that they feel too busy to complete themselves. Rather, the supervisor should invest time when setting tasks by carefully ensuring the task is suitable given the supervisee’s level of experience and that the supervisee has capacity to complete the task. The supervisor should also provide sufficient background information to allow the supervisee to make progress with the task and so that the supervisee understands the importance of the task in the context of the file as a whole.

Legal Aid Requirements

The Legal Services Commission of South Australia (“LSC”) is a statutory authority established by the Legal Services Commission Act 1977 (SA) (“the LSC Act”), to provide legal assistance to South Australians to enable them to navigate the legal system, which includes legal representation (s 10 of the LSC Act).
The provision of legal services can be provided either by a legal practitioner who is an officer of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia, or by a legal practitioner “assigned by the Commission for that purpose” (s 16 of the LSC Act).
The LSC maintains a register of practitioners who are approved by the Commission to provide legal services pursuant to such grants of legal aid. That register is known as the “panel” and consists of a “General Panel”, with subsections of “Civil”, “Criminal” and “Family” together with other specialist panels. Practitioners wishing to accept grants of legal aid in family law proceedings are members of the General Panel, and they are specifically approved to provide legal services on the “Family” sub-panel.
The published information on the LSC website makes clear that “a legal practitioner willing to undertake legal aid assignments (whether criminal, family or civil) must be a member of the Legal Services Commissions General Panel of practitioners” (Emphasis added).
That position is reinforced when regard is had to the “General Panel: Information and Application Form” published by the LSC (Exhibit “ICL2”), which clearly states:

On 26 February 2014 the Commission resolved that legal assistance cases will be granted only to practitioners accepted for inclusion on the General Panel.

(Emphasis added)
In addition, the “Minimum requirements for consideration for inclusion on the General Panel” set out in that document prescribe:
To be considered for inclusion on the General Panel a practitioner must –
a) Hold a current practising certificate;

b) Have at least two years experience in family law if wishing to receive assignments in family lay matters;

c) Provide a copy of a current DCSI screening clearance if wishing to receive assignments in which the client is a child.

(Emphasis added)
Practitioners who are approved onto the General Panel enter into a “General Panel Agreement” with the LSC (Exhibit “J1”). That document relevantly sets out the following:
2. It is agreed that as a member of the General Panel the legal practitioner-

...
2.2 will, if the practitioner holds a practising certificate with the restriction that the practitioner must complete supervised practice in accordance with rule 11 of the Rules of the Legal Practitioners Education and Admission Council 2018, comply with Legal Services’ guidelines specifying the type of case that may be undertaken by a practitioner holding a restricted practising certificate as published on Legal Services’ website from time to time;

2.3 will comply with the Legal Practitioners Act 1981, the Legal Services Commission Act 1977, the Australian Solicitors’ Conduct Rules adopted and amended by the Law Society of South Australia and, if applicable, the South Australian Barrister Conduct Rules;

2.4 will conduct a case for which legal assistance has been granted to the standard of care and skill expected of a person who regularly acts in the capacity in which the legal practitioner is engaged and who possesses the knowledge, skill and experience of a person qualified to act in that capacity;
...

(Bold emphasis added)

The “Practice Standards” attached to the General Panel Agreement relevantly set out:
The Legal Services Commission (Legal Services) has established a panel of private legal practitioners to provide legal services to legally assisted clients (the General Panel).

Clause 1.2 of the General Panel Agreement requires a legal practitioner included on the General Panel to provide services in a case for which legal assistance has been granted in accordance with the provisions of the General Panel Agreement, the General Practice Standards and any applicable specific practice standards, and any further conditions stipulated by the Director and attached to the Agreement.

A legal practitioner who breaches a practice standard may be suspended or removed from the General Panel and any other panel of which the practitioner is a member.

Legal Services has determined that the following general and specific practice standards are to be complied with by a legal practitioner representing a legally assisted client.

The General Practice Standards apply to all legally assisted cases. Specific practice standards have been developed to apply to particular types of cases and clients with specific characteristics.

The practice standards are designed to ensure the effective, efficient and economic delivery of quality legal services. They apply to both in-house legal practitioners and private legal practitioners acting on a grant of legal assistance.
The “General Practice Standards” set out in that document relevantly include:
A legal practitioner acting for a legally assisted client must-
...
attend all court hearings on time and adequately prepared;

...
A legal practitioner acting for a legally assisted client must-

...
be familiar with and observe the provisions of the funding criteria and other guidelines made pursuant to the Legal Services Commission Act 1977;

...
refuse the assignment of a case if the current workload of the legal practitioner is such that the case could not be attended to in a timely manner;

...
inform Legal Services of any concerns expressed by the court about the conduct of a legally assisted case;
notify Legal Services immediately of any circumstances that could give rise to a claim for professional negligence, including a detailed statement in relation to the matter;
maintain continuity of representation throughout the case to the extent possible and, where this is not possible, ensure that any practitioner to whom an appearance or other task is allocated is a member of the General Panel and, if relevant, a member of the appropriate specialist panel;

(Bold emphasis added)

The “guidelines” published on the LSC website under the heading “Restricted Practicing Certificates”, sets out the following:
The following criteria apply to practitioners holding restricted practising certificates:
Magistrates Court matters involving only summary charges may be assigned to a practitioner in the first year of a restricted practicing certificate. That practitioner may act as solicitor and counsel.
Magistrates Court matters involving only minor indictable charges, or a combination of minor indictable and summary charges, may be assigned to a practitioner in the second year of a restricted practicing certificate provided the Commission is informed of the nature of the matter, approves it to be undertaken by the named practitioner and is satisfied that the junior practitioner is properly supervised. That practitioner may act as solicitor and counsel
District and Supreme Court matters will only be assigned to a legal practitioner holding an unrestricted practicing certificate. The Commission acknowledges that on occasions junior practitioners working at a firm may be undertaking specific tasks on a legally aided file as a solicitor, but the Commission expects that the junior practitioner will be properly supervised in the conduct of these tasks. Counsel attendances in Court must be by a practitioner holding an unrestricted practicing certificate.
Matters arising under the Guardianship and Administration Act 1993 and the Mental Health Act 2009 may be assigned to a practitioner holding a restricted practising certificate to act as solicitor and counsel where:
the practitioner is a member of the Guardianship and Mental Health Representation Panel, and
the matter either:
is a review of an order under section 81 of the Mental Health Act 2009, or
is to be heard in the South Australian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (SACAT) in the exercise of its original jurisdiction, and
the Commission is satisfied that the practitioner will be properly supervised.
Practitioners should inform the Commission as soon as an unrestricted practising certificate is obtained.

DISCUSSION

The court understands from Mr O’Dea’s submissions that Mr E was admitted to practice in August 2025.
When regard is had to the “Practitioner Panel Registry” available on the LSC website, the court understands that Mr O’Dea is a member of the General Panel and is approved for each criminal, family and civil sub-panels, whereas Mr E is only approved for the criminal sub-panel. During the hearing on 9 February 2026, Mr O’Dea advised the court that Mr E was accepted as a member of the “Criminal” sub-panel of the General Panel on 9 January 2026; only some 19 days prior to his intended appearance at the hearing in these proceedings on 28 January 2026. The court was also informed at the hearing that Mr E had prepared the case outline filed in these proceedings on 21 January 2026.
When regard is had to all of the matters discussed herein:
(a) It is apparent that not only is Mr E not a member of the “Family” sub-panel of the General Panel, but he is also importantly ineligible to become a member as he has not practised family law for a period of two years, given he has only held a practicing certificate for six months; and

(b) There are no specific policies or guidelines issued by the LSC authorising the work or attendance of practitioners holding a restricted practicing certificate for appearances in any of the Federal Courts, be that the Federal Court of Australia, the Federal Circuit or Family Court of Australia, Division 1, or the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, Division 2.

Moreover, it is unsurprising that there are no such guidelines for authorising newly admitted practitioners who hold a restricted practising certificate to undertake family law work, given:
(a) Rule 11 of the LPEAC Rules, which provides that newly admitted practitioners are not permitted to hold an unrestricted practicing certificate unless they have completed supervised practice for a “continuous period of 2 years’ full time employment as an employed practitioner”; and

(b) The prerequisite for acceptance onto the “Family” sub-panel of the General Panel is that the practitioner must have at least two years experience in family law.

To that end, and when particular regard is had to paragraph 2(m) of the General Practice Standards earlier outlined in the LSC General Panel Agreement (Exhibit “J1”), it is apparent that Mr E is ineligible to undertake work and/or appear in family law proceedings pursuant to a grant of legal aid. Whilst Mr O’Dea argued that Mr E was permitted to appear in family law proceedings under his direct supervision as he is the holder of the grant of legal aid, that submission is rejected.
It therefore matters not what level of supervision Mr O’Dea has provided to Mr E, or indeed any other junior practitioners who he has regularly directed to undertake work and/or appear in family law proceedings pursuant to a grant of legal aid.
That being said, the level of supervision that Mr O’Dea appears to have provided Mr E warrants some comment. This is because it is conceivable that a junior practitioner holding a restricted practising certificate could be authorised by their supervising practitioner to appear in proceedings in this court (and other courts) where the client is privately funding their legal representation.
In the absence of further interrogation, the court for present purposes accepts Mr O’Dea’s submissions that he has supervised the solicitor work of Mr E, which included the preparation of the father’s Case Outline filed in these proceedings.
The court however does not accept that adequate supervision was provided so far as Mr E’s intended appearance in court in these proceedings on 28 January 2026 is concerned; particularly given his absence entirely from that hearing, which resulted in there being no representation whatsoever for the father, and the adjournment of the case management hearing.
What exactly constitutes “reasonable supervision” is nebulous and includes factors like:
(a) the experience of the practitioner;

(b) the requirements of each task assigned to that practitioner; and

(c) the practitioner’s level of experience gained during the supervision period.

The court however does not consider that reasonable supervision of a junior practitioner, of some six months post admission experience, includes a court appearance in the absence of direct supervision of the supervisee by the supervising practitioner at court.
The reason for this, in all of the circumstances of what occurred here, is obvious. Here the junior practitioner was entirely unaware (as was Mr O’Dea) of the obligations owed to the Legal Services Commission of South Australia pursuant to the terms of the grant of legal aid.
Moreover, the nature of any court hearing and/or attendance is that situations, questions and any manner of unforeseen circumstances, often arise. Unless the junior practitioner has gained a sufficient level of experience both generally and in appearing at court, they are unable to deal with those unforeseen situations, circumstances and questions because they do not have the experience, confidence and knowledge to do so.
The knock-on effect of this inability to deal with any such unforeseen situations, circumstances and questions is not simply a learning experience for the junior practitioner, but crucially it results in a direct consequence, likely negative, for the client for whom the junior practitioner is appearing. It should not require emphasis, however it must be remembered that legal practitioners represent clients. The inability of a legal practitioner to competently do so ultimately is not only a breach of a solicitor’s professional obligations, but importantly it invariably leads to a diminution in the confidence that the public has in the legal profession.
As the South Australian Legal Practitioners Conduct Rules 2022 (SA) emphasise:
PARAMOUNT DUTY TO THE COURT AND THE ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE
3.1 A legal practitioner’s duty to the court and the administration of justice is paramount and prevails to the extent of inconsistency with any other duty.
4. OTHER FUNDAMENTAL ETHICAL DUTIES

4.1 A legal practitioner must also:
4.1.1 act in the best interests of a client in any matter in which the practitioner represents the client;

4.1.2 be honest and courteous in all dealings in the course of legal practice;

4.1.3 deliver legal services competently, diligently and as promptly as reasonably possible;

4.1.4 avoid any compromise to their integrity and professional independence; and

4.1.5 comply with these Rules and the law.
As earlier identified, but warranting repetition, the LPEAC guidelines for supervised practice (under the sub-heading “2.2. Supervised practice: a developmental context”) make clear the:
... period of supervised practice is a unique supervisory context characterised by a supervisee who has limited practical experience. In this context, the supervisee’s professional development is critical and relies on specific training and support measures enabling a transition to unrestricted practice. This requires both the supervisor and the supervisee to have realistic expectations about what knowledge and skill set the supervisee is equipped with upon admission as a legal practitioner. In particular, supervisees at the beginning of their supervised practice period will have very limited legal practice experience and are in need of tailored training that progressively develops their level of competence.
The effect of the LPEAC guidelines make it clear that the days of supervising practitioners throwing a junior practitioner to the wind are long gone; particularly the days of having junior practitioners appear in court freshly post admission to practice.
“Supervision” as the name implies, mandates that a senior practitioner will actually “supervise” the junior practitioner.
The Oxford English Dictionary defines “supervision” as:
The action or function of overseeing, directing, or taking charge of a person, organization, activity etc.
With that in mind, conceivably in person supervision at court may foreseeably be capable of lessening as a junior practitioner comes to the end of their restricted term, but that will always be informed in the particular case of each junior practitioner on the knowledge, skill and practical experience the supervisee has gained during the period of supervision.
It is for all of these reasons that the court considers it appropriate to refer the conduct of Mr O’Dea to both the Legal Profession Conduct Commissioner and the Director of the Legal Services Commission of South Australia.
It is also for all of these reasons that the court considers it appropriate to make orders for Mr O’Dea to pay personally the costs thrown away of both the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer for the hearing which was unable to proceed on 28 January 2026; noting that they are each also funded by grants of legal aid and the limited resource of legal aid funding ought not be consumed by costs which should not have been incurred.

ADDITIONAL CONCERNS

A further issue warrants comment in the context of these proceedings arising from matters the court is aware of, and Mr O’Dea acknowledged at the hearing on 9 February 2026; namely that in the shadow of the court having listed these proceedings for a show cause event by virtue of the orders made on 28 January 2026:
(a) On 29 January 2026, being the very next day, Mr E sought to appear in separate proceedings before me pursuant to a grant of legal aid. Whilst Mr O’Dea was present in the body of the court room on that occasion, in light of the show cause hearing that had been listed in these proceedings, it was misguided and imprudent for Mr O’Dea to have done so without having first obtained either clarity from the LSC and/or the court, neither of which had occurred.

(b) Staggeringly, a week later in separate proceedings before Judge Dickson in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), the very circumstance that confronted the court in these proceedings on 28 January 2026 was repeated, with Mr E attempting to appear unsupervised and without Mr O’Dea pursuant to a grant of legal aid. That situation led to Judge Dickson refusing to hear Mr E and ultimately making a personal costs order against Mr O’Dea (McNeil & Rydell [2026] FedCFamC2F 149).
